MMill Developer Guide · v1

For developers who write JavaScript

Build and ship a workflow
without touching Kubernetes.

Mill turns a graph of small JavaScript steps into a service that runs on a schedule, a webhook, or on demand. You write the JS. Mill runs it.

✓

You do not need EKS, kubectl, or cluster access to use Mill. Everything in this guide happens in the app (or by committing to a git repo). When something breaks, you see it in the dashboard — not in a pod log you can't reach.

The whole model in three sentences

where code lives

A project is a git repo

Your team's workflows live in one repository. Mill watches it and keeps the running system matching what's committed.

what you build

A workflow is a graph of steps

Each step is a plain .js file. Boxes are steps; arrows are the order. An If branches; a Loop repeats; Call Script reuses another workflow.

how you ship

Save is a git commit

There's no separate "deploy". You Save, Mill commits it, and the reconciler rolls the change out. What you see is what runs is what's in git.

01

Find your project, then your workflow

Workspace → a project → a workflow

Open Mill in your browser. The Workspace lists every project (each is a git repo) with its sync and health at a glance — green means the live system matches git.

Click a project to see its workflows, each with its triggers and last-run status. Pick one to open it.

02

Read the graph

The editor: canvas on the left, inspector on the right

A workflow is a real program drawn as a flow. Here, Nightly Invoices fetches invoices, transforms them, branches on whether any are open, and loads the warehouse. Click any box to inspect it; drag a component from the palette to add one.

03

Write a step

A JS Code node is one .js file

Select a JS Code node and open its editor. Every step is a default-exported async function that receives the previous step's output as input and a ctx helper, and returns the next step's input:

// nodes/transform.js — a step is just this
export default async function transform(input, ctx) {
  ctx.log.info("transforming", { count: input.rows.length });
  const total = input.rows.reduce((n, r) => n + r.amount, 0);
  return { total, currency: "USD" };   // → becomes the next step's input
}

ctx gives you ctx.log (structured logs), ctx.secrets.NAME (injected secrets, never in git), ctx.state (survives retries and loop iterations), and ctx.request (the raw webhook payload, headers, and query). Edit, then Save & Apply to stage the change as a draft.

04

Use an npm package

Declare a dependency — Mill installs it for you

Need date-fns, zod, or an SDK? Add it in the node's Dependencies panel. On Save, Mill installs it into the project so both in-process and isolated runs resolve it — and it's written into the exported bundle's package.json too. No build step to wire up.

05

Test one step in isolation

Run a single node with an input you choose

You don't have to run the whole workflow to check a step. Give Test this step a JSON input and press Run step — Mill executes just that node against the live backend and shows the output and logs. Great for nailing a step's shape before you wire it up.

Try the edges

Send {}, null, or a wrong type on purpose. A well-written step either handles it or fails cleanly with a message — no silent surprises later.

06

Decide how it runs

Triggers, schedule, webhook URL — and a couple of safety valves

The Triggers panel is where a workflow becomes a service. Switch a trigger's type and the right field appears:

  • Manual — run it yourself from the editor or the API.
  • Cron — a schedule like 0 2 * * * (daily at 02:00).
  • Webhook — Mill gives you a tokenized URL to POST to; copy it with one click.

Two optional safety valves live here too: Run exclusively (this run gets a whole worker to itself — for heavy jobs) and a Run input schema (a one-line JS check that rejects malformed payloads at the door).

# Call a webhook workflow from anywhere (bearer token required)
curl -X POST https://mill.your-co.com/p/w/intake/acuity \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -d '{"email":"a@co.com","items":[1,2]}'
# add ?wait=1 to get the result back synchronously (bounded ~30s)
07

Run the whole thing

Press Run, watch it stream

Hit Run with a sample input. Each node lights up as it executes, logs stream in live, and the run reports Succeeded or fails on the exact node that threw — with the error attached.

Retries are built in

Give a flaky step retry: { maxAttempts: 3, backoffMs: 500 } and Mill retries it with backoff. Completed steps are journaled, so a retried run skips the work it already finished.

08

Ship it — Save is a commit

Your change becomes a git commit; Mill rolls it out

When it works, press Save. Mill shows you exactly what will change — the workflow definition and the node files — as a diff, you write a commit message, and it commits to the repo. The reconciler then syncs the running system to match. That's the whole deploy.

Prefer to work in git?

You can. Edit the same workflow.yaml and nodes/*.js in the repo and push — Mill picks it up on the next reconcile (or instantly, if a push webhook is wired). The UI and git are two doors into the same room.

09

Watch it run — and see failures

The Fleet dashboard is your window into production

The Fleet page shows the workers running your jobs, the queue, throughput, and — front and center — a red banner and counter the moment runs start failing. You don't kubectl logs anything; the failing workflows and their errors surface here.

You & Kubernetes: who owns what

You were told you might not have full EKS access. Good news: you don't need it. Here's the clean split.

yours — no cluster access needed

The workflow

The JS steps, the graph, the triggers, secrets references, retries and schemas — all authored in the app or committed to the repo. You test, run, and ship entirely from here.

platform / ops — set up once

The cluster

The EKS deployment, secret values, autoscaling, and the git connection are configured by whoever runs the platform. You consume it; you don't operate it.

The one thing to ask your platform team for

The secret names your steps can use (you reference them as ctx.secrets.NAME; the values are injected in the cluster, never in git) and your project's webhook token if you're wiring external systems.

Quick reference

Node kinds

KindWhat it does
StartEntry point — receives the run input.
JS CodeA step backed by its own .js file.
IfBranches the flow on a condition (true / false edges).
LoopRuns a body per item of an array; collects results.
Call ScriptInvokes another workflow as a step.
FanoutCalls 1–N downstream workflows in parallel.
EndExit — returns the result to the trigger.

Triggers

TypeFires when
manualYou run it (editor / API).
cronA schedule matches, e.g. */15 * * * *.
webhookSomething POSTs your tokenized URL.
eventAnother workflow calls it.
🧰 The ctx helper
ctx.log.info(msg, obj)Structured logs → the run's live feed.
ctx.secrets.NAMEA secret value, injected by the cluster.
ctx.stateMutable bag that survives retries & loop items.
ctx.requestRaw webhook body, headers, query.
🛡️ Guardrails you can add
Per-step retryretry: { maxAttempts, backoffMs }
Step I/O checkinputSchema / outputSchema
Whole-run input checkRun input schema (Triggers panel)
No overlap (cron)concurrencyPolicy: Forbid | Replace
Dedicated workerRun exclusively (toggle)
If Save is blocked

Mill won't ship a workflow whose code won't parse — you'll get a clear "won't compile" message naming the node. Fix the syntax and Save again.

Mill — Developer Guide Write JavaScript · Save · it runs. You never touch the cluster.
